text: The surgeon identifies a 7x6 cm intraparietal hernia extending through the transversus abdominis and internal oblique muscles, while the external oblique muscle remains intact. Moderate lysis of adhesions is performed, revealing the abdominal wall defect in the right abdomen.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal plane starting on the left side of the midline and extends it across the midline. On the right side, the posterior rectus sheath is incised to enter the retrorectus plane, which is extended laterally. The lateral posterior rectus sheath is then incised, allowing re-entry into the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal plane in the area of the abdominal wall defect, carefully separating the peritoneum to reveal the intact external oblique muscle.
The surgeon successfully releases the peritoneum completely from the area of weakness in the abdominal wall, revealing the underlying structures.

The surgeon dissects over the top of the transplanted kidney to create lateral overlap for mesh placement. After achieving the necessary overlap, the fascial defect is closed with a running number one Stratafix barbed suture.
The surgeon inserts an 18 by 5 centimeters soft barred mesh over the repaired area, securing it with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl sutures to reinforce the primary repair.

A 5mm trocar is inserted into the right upper quadrant, facilitating the placement of a 19 French Blake drain into the extraperitoneal space adjacent to the mesh. The surgeon then closes the peritoneum using a running 2-0 V-lock barbed suture.
The surgical drain is opened to the air, allowing the carbon dioxide in the extraperitoneal space to be evacuated.
